eviction.court_process_summary_rule: {"statutes":"68 P.S. §§ 250.601, 250.602; Pa. Rules of Civil Procedure 1910.12–1910.19","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"10-day notice to pay or vacate for residential tenants (Act 54 of 2024 extended from 7 to 10 days)","holdover_tenant":"30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy","lease_violation":"30-day notice for noncompliance under the act","no_cause":"Philadelphia covered units require just cause (Bill No. 240276, effective 2025)"},"full_text":"68 P.S. § 250.601. Landlord's remedies for breach. If the tenant fails to comply with the tenant's obligations under this act or the rental agreement, the landlord may: (1) Terminate the tenancy and bring an action for possession; (2) Recover damages caused by the tenant's breach; (3) Bring an action for rent that is due.\n68 P.S. § 250.602. Recovery of possession limited. No landlord may recover possession of a dwelling unit unless the landlord: (1) Has a court order for possession; (2) Complies with all applicable laws regarding the eviction process; (3) Gives proper notice to the tenant as required by law.\nPennsylvania eviction procedures are governed by Pa. Rules of Civil Procedure 1910.12–1910.19. A landlord must serve a notice to quit (10 days for nonpayment under Act 54 of 2024) before filing a complaint in ejectment. The court will schedule a hearing and issue a judgment. If the judgment is for the landlord, a writ of possession may be issued."}

{"statutes":"68 P.S. §§ 250.101, 250.102, 250.201, 250.511a, 250.512","full_text":"68 P.S. § 250.101. Definitions. 'Security deposit' means any payment of money given to a landlord by or on behalf of a tenant to secure the performance of any obligation of the tenant under a rental agreement. 'Tenant' means a person who is a lessee in possession of premises. 'Landlord' means the owner or manager of a rental premises.
68 P.S. § 250.102. Implied warranty of habitability. In any written or oral rental agreement for a dwelling unit, there is an implied warranty that the premises are and will remain fit for human habitation. This warranty may not be waived.
68 P.S. § 250.201. Rental agreement. (a) A rental agreement may contain any terms and conditions not prohibited by this act or other law, including terms providing for the establishment of a security deposit. (b) A security deposit may not exceed two months' rent for the first year and one month's rent for renewals, except in Philadelphia where different limits may apply.
68 P.S. § 250.511a. Escrow funds limited. (a) A landlord who receives a security deposit shall deposit the money in a fiduciary capacity in a banking institution in Pennsylvania, to be held in trust for the deposit of the tenant. (b) Within thirty (30) days of the termination of the tenant's occupancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any deductions. (c) The landlord shall give the tenant an itemized list of deductions and the balance due.
68 P.S. § 250.512. Recovery of improperly held escrow funds. (a) If a landlord fails to return the security deposit within thirty (30) days, the tenant may recover the full amount of the security deposit as a debt in an action brought in the court of common pleas. (b) If the landlord fails to return the security deposit in bad faith, the tenant may recover treble damages."}

sources.title: {"statutes":"68 P.S. §§ 250.601, 250.602; Pa. Rules of Civil Procedure 1910.12–1910.19","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"10-day notice to pay or vacate for residential tenants (Act 54 of 2024 extended from 7 to 10 days)","holdover_tenant":"30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy","lease_violation":"30-day notice for noncompliance under the act","no_cause":"Philadelphia covered units require just cause (Bill No. 240276, effective 2025)"},"full_text":"68 P.S. § 250.601. Landlord's remedies for breach. If the tenant fails to comply with the tenant's obligations under this act or the rental agreement, the landlord may: (1) Terminate the tenancy and bring an action for possession; (2) Recover damages caused by the tenant's breach; (3) Bring an action for rent that is due.\n68 P.S. § 250.602. Recovery of possession limited. No landlord may recover possession of a dwelling unit unless the landlord: (1) Has a court order for possession; (2) Complies with all applicable laws regarding the eviction process; (3) Gives proper notice to the tenant as required by law.\nPennsylvania eviction procedures are governed by Pa. Rules of Civil Procedure 1910.12–1910.19. A landlord must serve a notice to quit (10 days for nonpayment under Act 54 of 2024) before filing a complaint in ejectment. The court will schedule a hearing and issue a judgment. If the judgment is for the landlord, a writ of possession may be issued."}

sources.title: {"statutes":"68 P.S. § 250.104","full_text":"68 P.S. § 250.104. Landlord's right of entry. (a) A landlord may enter the tenant's premises without the tenant's consent in case of emergency. (b) For non-emergency entry, a landlord may enter the tenant's premises at reasonable times upon reasonable notice if: (1) The landlord gives the tenant advance notice; (2) The entry is for a legitimate purpose such as inspection, repair, or showing to prospective tenants or buyers; (3) The entry is at a reasonable time. Pennsylvania courts have generally recognized that reasonable notice means at least 24 hours. (c) The tenant shall not unreasonably withhold consent for the landlord to enter."}

sources.title: {"statutes":"68 P.S. §§ 250.102, 250.103","full_text":"68 P.S. § 250.102. Implied warranty of habitability. In any written or oral rental agreement for a dwelling unit, there is an implied warranty that the premises are and will remain fit for human habitation. This warranty may not be waived.\n68 P.S. § 250.103. Landlord's duties. The landlord shall: (1) Comply with all applicable building and housing codes materially affecting health and safety; (2) Make all repairs necessary to keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (3) Keep all common areas in a safe and sanitary condition; (4) Maintain all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, and air conditioning fixtures and appliances in good working order; (5) Provide running water and reasonable hot water and heat unless the building is not required by law to have these facilities or the utilities are under the tenant's exclusive control.\nIf the landlord fails to comply with the implied warranty of habitability, the tenant may: (a) Bring an action for damages; (b) Withhold rent and deposit it in court; (c) Terminate the lease; (d) Make repairs and deduct the cost from rent (limited to the lesser of the cost of repairs or one month's rent)."}
